beats.narration_text: Konnichiwa — this is Liam, in for Bear. Ask Claude to create an agent, add a subagent, write agent frontmatter, or get guidance on triggering conditions — the Agent Development skill fires. Agents are autonomous subprocesses that handle complex, multi-step tasks independently. They live in a markdown file with YAML frontmatter — name, description, model, color, tools — and a system prompt body. The description field is the most critical: it determines when the harness dispatches the agent. This version uses a two-location trigger approach: a prose summary in the description plus a dedicated When to invoke section in the body. Let me walk you through the anatomy, the trigger design, and where this skill gets it right and where it bites.

props.command: Create an agent for my plugin that handles code review tasks.
output: AGENT.md: YAML frontmatter (name, description, model, color, tools) + system prompt body
Description: prose summary of triggers + pointer to 'When to invoke' body section
When to invoke: 2-4 prose bullet scenarios in the agent body

beats.act: anatomy — agent file structure + frontmatter fields
beats.narration_text: An agent file has YAML frontmatter and a markdown system prompt body. Five frontmatter fields. Name: required, lowercase with hyphens, three to fifty characters, starts and ends with alphanumeric — code-reviewer, test-generator, security-analyzer. Description: the most critical field, loaded into context whenever the agent is registered so the harness decides when to dispatch; in this version it uses a prose format with trigger conditions and a pointer to a When to invoke section. Model: inherit uses the parent model, which is the recommended default. Color: visual distinction in the Claude interface — blue, green, red, yellow, orange, purple, pink, or cyan. Tools: the array of tool permissions the agent gets — give it only what it needs, Read and Grep for analysis-only agents, add Write and Edit only if the task requires changes. The system prompt body defines the agent's role, responsibilities, workflow, and output format. Include a When to invoke section at the top of the body with two to four prose-bullet scenarios describing what the situation looks like and what the agent should do.

props.sparkLine: Frontmatter triggers. Body becomes the system prompt. When to invoke in both.

beats.act: design — two-location trigger approach + description format
beats.narration_text: The trigger design in this version uses two locations. Location one: the description field. Format: Use this agent when [conditions]. Typical triggers include [scenario 1 in prose], [scenario 2 in prose], and [scenario 3 in prose]. See When to invoke in the agent body for worked scenarios. The prose summary names two to four trigger scenarios, covers both proactive and reactive triggering, covers different phrasings of the same intent, and is specific about when NOT to use the agent. Location two: the body's When to invoke section. Format: bold scenario name followed by what the situation looks like and what the agent should do — two to four prose bullets. The two locations serve different audiences: the description is for the harness dispatch decision, kept short. The body section is for the agent itself once invoked, providing detailed worked scenarios. Four things that make a good system prompt: a concrete role statement, numbered responsibilities, a step-by-step analysis process, and a specified output format. Keep the system prompt focused and scoped — an agent that tries to do everything will do nothing well.

props.sparkLine: Prose in description. Prose in body. Two locations, two audiences, one maintenance burden.

beats.act: teardown — what it gets right / where it bites
beats.narration_text: Here is the teardown. Agent Development gets the file structure right: five frontmatter fields documented with concrete constraints — name pattern, model options, color choices, tools list. The two-location approach is explicitly motivated: the description is for the harness, the When to invoke section is for the agent. The name field constraints are complete: lowercase, hyphens only, three to fifty characters, starts and ends with alphanumeric, with good and bad examples. The tools principle is correct: give agents only the permissions they need — Read and Grep for analysis, not Write unless the task requires it. And the system prompt structure — role statement, responsibilities, analysis process, output format — is a workable template. Where it bites: the two-location approach creates maintenance coupling. If the trigger scenarios evolve, both the description prose and the When to invoke section need to be updated consistently; there's no enforcement. Prose scenarios are less machine-parseable than structured XML examples — the harness uses the description for dispatch, so prose is harder to use as canonical dispatch examples than structured markup. The trigger mechanism itself is still unexplained: does the harness pattern-match the description or use language model judgment? Model inherit is recommended but no guidance on when to override, or what to choose for long-context versus reasoning-heavy tasks. And there's no guidance on system prompt length constraints.

props.sparkLine: Two-location trigger docs readable. Maintenance coupling: consolidate.

beats.narration_text: Verdict. Agent Development fires for creating agents, writing agent frontmatter, trigger conditions, system prompts. Structure: YAML frontmatter (name, description, model, color, tools) + system prompt body. Two-location triggers: prose summary in description + When to invoke prose bullets in body. Description format: Use this agent when... Typical triggers include... See When to invoke. System prompt: role + responsibilities + process + output format. Gaps: two-location maintenance coupling; prose less parseable than examples; trigger mechanism unexplained; model override guidance absent.

artifactLines: AGENT.md: name · description · model · color · tools + system prompt body
Two-location triggers: prose in description + When to invoke section in body
Description: 'Use when... Typical triggers include... See When to invoke.'
System prompt: role · responsibilities · analysis process · output format
Tools: minimum permissions — Read/Grep only unless Write is required
Gaps: two-location coupling · prose vs examples · trigger mechanism unexplained

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Open a Claude Code session. Paste this: Create an agent for my plugin that reviews pull request diffs for security issues. Watch four things. First: does the description use the prose format — Use this agent when... Typical triggers include... See When to invoke? Second: does the body include a When to invoke section with two to four prose-bullet scenarios — bold scenario name followed by what the situation looks like? Third: are tools restricted to the minimum necessary — probably Read and Bash for diff inspection, not Write? And fourth: does the system prompt have a concrete role statement, numbered responsibilities, a step-by-step process, and an output format — not just 'you are a security reviewer'? If the description has no When to invoke pointer — the body section is orphaned. If the body has no When to invoke section — the description pointer is broken. Both must exist and stay synchronized. That is your maintenance gate.

props.command: Create an agent for my plugin that reviews pull request diffs for security issues.
props.runningText: Watch: prose description? When to invoke section? minimum tools? full system prompt?
output: Expected: description prose format + pointer to 'When to invoke' in body
Expected: body has When to invoke with 2-4 bold-scenario prose bullets
Red flag: no When to invoke section · Write tool without need · generic role statement
